notes: M1/M2 microglia polarization is contested; spectrum model preferred (PMC10676790).
QIF band assignments are functional estimates, not validated mappings.
All BCI relevance statements are for threat modeling purposes.
Quantitative model linking gliosis progression to Cs degradation is a research gap.

glial_types.name: Astrocytes

subtypes.name: Protoplasmic
subtypes.morphology: Dense tortuous GFAP+ processes; domain organization (non-overlapping)
subtypes.primary_function: Synaptic modulation, BBB maintenance, K+ buffering

subtypes.name: Fibrous
subtypes.morphology: Fewer foot processes than protoplasmic; larger soma

subtypes.name: Interlaminar
subtypes.morphology: Long vertical cable-like processes (up to 1 mm) connecting layers I to III-IV

subtypes.name: Varicose projection
subtypes.morphology: 1-5 long processes (up to 1 mm), spiny branches, varicosities every 10 um

subtypes.name: Reactive
subtypes.primary_function: Scar formation; pro- and anti-inflammatory roles

functions: BBB maintenance via end-feet TGF-beta/GDNF/Ang1 signaling to endothelial cells
Glutamate-glutamine cycle (GLAST/GLT-1 uptake -> GS -> glutamine export to neurons via SNAT3/5)
K+ spatial buffering via Kir4.1 + AQP4 co-localization + gap junction syncytium redistribution
Tripartite synapse: mGluR-mediated Ca2+ rise triggers gliotransmitter release (glutamate, D-serine, ATP, GABA)
D-serine release required for hippocampal LTP induction (NMDAR glycine-site co-agonism)
Synaptogenesis via thrombospondin (TSP1/2) and glypican (GPC4/6) release
Lactate shuttle to neurons (ANLS) via MCT4 export
pH regulation via carbonic anhydrase (CO2 -> HCO3-)

molecular_markers.name: GFAP
molecular_markers.notes: Primary pan-astrocyte marker; intermediate filament; upregulated in reactive gliosis

molecular_markers.name: S100B
molecular_markers.notes: Ca2+-binding protein; predominantly protoplasmic (gray matter); absent in fibrous (white matter)

molecular_markers.name: AQP4
molecular_markers.notes: Water channel; concentrated on vascular end-feet; glymphatic function

molecular_markers.name: GLAST/EAAT1
molecular_markers.notes: Glutamate transporter; expressed near excitatory synapses

molecular_markers.name: GLT-1/EAAT2
molecular_markers.notes: Major glutamate transporter (~80% synaptic glutamate clearance)

molecular_markers.name: Connexin 43
molecular_markers.notes: Gap junction protein; astrocyte syncytium communication

molecular_markers.name: Connexin 30
molecular_markers.notes: Gap junction protein; subtype-specific

molecular_markers.name: Kir4.1
molecular_markers.notes: Inwardly-rectifying K+ channel; K+ spatial buffering

molecular_markers.name: Aldh1L1
molecular_markers.notes: Broadly expressed pan-astrocyte marker; useful when GFAP is low

molecular_markers.name: Thrombospondin-1/2
molecular_markers.notes: Released by astrocytes; promote synaptogenesis

molecular_markers.name: Glypican-4/6
molecular_markers.notes: Released by astrocytes; synapse formation/maturation

calcium_signaling.mechanism: IP3-mediated intracellular Ca2+ via mGluR activation
calcium_signaling.bold_coupling: Evoked Ca2+ -> positive BOLD; intrinsic Ca2+ oscillations -> negative BOLD

glial_types.security_implications: Reactive gliosis at I0 is primary chronic BCI failure mode. GFAP upregulation is a measurable biomarker of implant-induced disruption. Calcium waves represent slow intercellular signaling not captured by electrode LFP. Gliotransmission modulation could alter synaptic plasticity without direct neuronal stimulation (inferred threat vector, not demonstrated).

glial_types.name: Oligodendrocytes

subtypes.name: OPC (Oligodendrocyte Precursor Cell)
subtypes.location: Throughout CNS, gray and white matter
subtypes.notes: Proliferative precursors; among first responders to electrode implantation (within 1 week)

subtypes.name: Immature oligodendrocyte
subtypes.notes: Transitional stage; committed to oligodendrocyte lineage

subtypes.name: Mature myelinating oligodendrocyte
subtypes.notes: Each cell myelinates up to 60 axon segments simultaneously

functions: CNS myelin production (up to 60 axon segments per cell)
Saltatory conduction: electrical insulation between nodes of Ranvier
Metabolic support to ensheathed axons
Axon-glial signaling via MAG/periaxonal membrane

molecular_markers.name: NG2
molecular_markers.notes: OPC marker; chondroitin sulfate proteoglycan

molecular_markers.name: PDGFRa
molecular_markers.notes: OPC marker; platelet-derived growth factor receptor alpha

molecular_markers.name: Olig2
molecular_markers.notes: Transcription factor; OPC and immature OL

molecular_markers.name: O4
molecular_markers.notes: Immature OL marker

molecular_markers.name: CC1
molecular_markers.notes: Mature OL marker

myelin_proteins.name: MBP (Myelin Basic Protein)
myelin_proteins.disease_association: Marker of mature OL; intrinsically disordered protein

myelin_proteins.name: CNP
myelin_proteins.function: Early OL marker; membrane maintenance; RNA metabolism

glial_types.security_implications: Demyelination (MS, implant microtrauma) slows conduction velocity and alters signal timing, degrading coherence metric transport variance. FUS-mediated BBB opening protocols must avoid thermal/cavitation damage to white matter myelin. OPCs (NG2+) are among first responders to electrode implantation within 1 week.

glial_types.name: Microglia
glial_types.origin: Yolk-sac macrophage precursors (not bone marrow); colonize brain during embryonic development

subtypes.name: Homeostatic (surveilling)
subtypes.notes: Contact every synapse approximately every hour

subtypes.name: Disease-associated microglia (DAM)
subtypes.notes: Identified via single-cell RNA sequencing; associated with neurodegeneration

glial_types.m1_m2_polarization_note: CONTESTED. M1 (pro-inflammatory) and M2 (anti-inflammatory) dichotomy is now considered an oversimplification. Single-cell RNA sequencing reveals a continuous spectrum of context-, region-, and time-dependent states. Use homeostatic, reactive, or state-specific descriptors rather than M1/M2. Source: PMC10676790.
functions: Constant surveillance via dynamic process extension/retraction
Synaptic pruning: C1q marking -> C3 activation -> C3b opsonization -> CR3 (CD11b/CD18) phagocytosis
Trophic support: BDNF, IGF-1 production
Clearance of debris, dead cells, misfolded proteins
Cytokine release: pro-inflammatory (IL-1beta, IL-6, TNFalpha, MCP-1/CCL2, ROS, NO via iNOS)
Cytokine release: anti-inflammatory/repair (IL-10, TGF-beta, IGF-1)

molecular_markers.name: IBA1
molecular_markers.notes: Actin-bundling protein; pan-microglia; upregulated in activated states; enables phagocytosis

molecular_markers.name: TMEM119
molecular_markers.notes: Highly specific to homeostatic microglia; downregulated in activated/reactive states

molecular_markers.name: P2RY12
molecular_markers.notes: Purinergic receptor; homeostatic marker; senses ADP from injury

molecular_markers.name: CX3CR1
molecular_markers.notes: Fractalkine receptor; neuron-microglia communication via CX3CL1 on neurons

molecular_markers.name: CD68
molecular_markers.notes: Lysosomal marker; phagocytic activity indicator

molecular_markers.name: CD11b
molecular_markers.notes: Complement receptor 3 (CR3) beta chain; phagocytosis of C3-opsonized synapses

molecular_markers.name: HexB
molecular_markers.notes: Pan-microglia marker independent of activation state

events: Microglia proliferation and migration to electrode
Cytokine storm: IL-1beta, IL-6, TNFalpha, MCP-1 (CCL2)
Neuron loss from 50-100 um perimeter around electrode
Negative correlation between non-neuronal density (NND) and recording quality

signal_degradation.acute: Daily amplitude shifts within first week (micromotion)
signal_degradation.chronic: Glial encapsulation increases electrode-tissue impedance; reactive glia shift toward inhibitory tone

mitigation_strategies.strategy: Feature sizes <10 um (below cellular attachment threshold)

glial_types.security_implications: Foreign body response at I0 (electrode-tissue interface) is THE primary chronic failure mode for implanted BCIs. Microglia-mediated signal degradation directly impairs Cs over time by increasing transport variance (signal path integrity degradation) and gain variance (impedance rise, amplitude fall). Quantitative model linking gliosis progression to Cs degradation is NOT YET DEVELOPED -- this is a research gap.

glial_types.name: Ependymal Cells

subtypes.name: Standard ependymal
subtypes.location: Ventricular lining (all four ventricles) and central canal of spinal cord
subtypes.morphology: Simple columnar (cuboidal in some regions); ~50 motile cilia + microvilli on apical surface
subtypes.primary_function: CSF propulsion via synchronous ciliary beating

subtypes.name: Choroid plexus epithelial (modified ependymal)
subtypes.location: Choroid plexus in lateral, third, and fourth ventricles
subtypes.morphology: Cuboidal; larger microvilli; fenestrated capillaries; Na+/K+-ATPase enrichment
subtypes.primary_function: Active CSF secretion (~80% of total CSF production)

subtypes.name: Tanycytes
subtypes.morphology: Long basal processes extending to hypothalamic nuclei and median eminence
subtypes.primary_function: Chemosensing; transport between CSF and hypothalamic neurons; neuroendocrine regulation

functions: Synchronous ciliary beating propels CSF through ventricular system (lateral -> 3rd -> 4th -> subarachnoid)
Choroid plexus subtype: active CSF secretion via Na+/K+-ATPase driven transport (~80% of 400-600 mL/day)
Blood-CSF barrier function via tight junctions (choroid plexus)
Neural stem cell niche: adult SVZ ependymal cells contribute to neurogenesis (primarily rodents; less clear in humans)

molecular_markers.name: S100B
molecular_markers.notes: Shared with astrocytes; expressed in ependyma

molecular_markers.name: Vimentin
molecular_markers.notes: Intermediate filament; ependymal and radial glia

molecular_markers.name: FoxJ1
molecular_markers.notes: Transcription factor for motile ciliogenesis

molecular_markers.name: CD24
molecular_markers.notes: Glycoprotein; ependymal surface marker

molecular_markers.name: Na+/K+-ATPase
molecular_markers.notes: Enriched in choroid plexus subtype; drives CSF secretion

structural_features.blood_csf_barrier: Choroid plexus tight junctions form blood-CSF barrier (distinct from BBB)

glial_types.clinical_relevance: Loss of ependymal cilia function (primary ciliary dyskinesia) causes hydrocephalus in animal models. Ependymal injury from ventriculitis can cause hydrocephalus in humans.
glial_types.security_implications: Ependymal cells regulate CSF pressure and composition, which indirectly affects all N-band structures via intracranial pressure dynamics. Disrupted ependymal function would alter the CSF microenvironment that all N-band neural tissue depends on. Indirect relevance to BCI signal quality through ICP changes.

glial_types.name: Schwann Cells

subtypes.name: Myelinating Schwann cell
subtypes.morphology: Compact myelin wrapping; 1:1 ratio (one Schwann cell per one axon segment)

subtypes.name: Non-myelinating (Remak) Schwann cell
subtypes.morphology: Envelops multiple small axons without compact myelin
subtypes.primary_function: Structural and metabolic support of unmyelinated axons

functions: PNS myelin production (1:1 cell-to-axon-segment ratio)
Saltatory conduction in peripheral nerves
Trophic support to PNS axons
Post-injury: dedifferentiation, proliferation, Bungner band formation for axon regeneration guidance
Remyelination of regenerating axons

molecular_markers.name: S100
molecular_markers.notes: Pan-Schwann cell marker

molecular_markers.name: SOX10
molecular_markers.notes: Transcription factor; neural crest-derived glia

molecular_markers.name: EGR2/Krox20
molecular_markers.notes: Transcription factor required for myelination

molecular_markers.name: MPZ/P0
molecular_markers.notes: Major PNS myelin protein; myelinating Schwann cells only

molecular_markers.name: PMP22
molecular_markers.notes: PNS myelin; myelinating Schwann cells

pns_myelin_proteins.name: P0 (MPZ)
pns_myelin_proteins.function: Major structural protein; homophilic adhesion compacts extracellular leaflets

pns_myelin_proteins.name: PMP22
pns_myelin_proteins.disease_association: CMT1A (most common hereditary neuropathy; gene duplication)

pns_myelin_proteins.name: MBP (PNS isoforms)
pns_myelin_proteins.function: Cytoplasmic leaflet compaction (shared with CNS but different dominant isoforms)

glial_types.key_distinction: One Schwann cell myelinates one axon segment (1:1 ratio) vs. one oligodendrocyte myelinates up to 60 segments in CNS.

regeneration.mechanism: Schwann cells dedifferentiate, proliferate, form Bungner bands to guide regenerating axons, then remyelinate
regeneration.contrast_with_cns: PNS injuries show better functional recovery than CNS white matter injuries due to Schwann cell regenerative capacity

glial_types.security_implications: Peripheral/spinal BCI interfaces (Utah arrays in peripheral nerve, DRG stimulators, spinal cord stimulators) interact with Schwann cells. Peripheral nerve electrode foreign body response involves Schwann cell displacement and fibrous encapsulation, qualitatively similar to CNS gliosis but with greater regenerative potential. Relevant for peripheral BCI configurations tapping at spinal level before signals enter the I0 zone.

glial_types.name: Satellite Glial Cells

subtypes.name: Satellite glial cell (standard)
subtypes.location: DRG (dorsal root ganglia), trigeminal ganglia, sympathetic and parasympathetic ganglia
subtypes.morphology: Tight, complete sheath around each ganglion neuron soma; gap-junction connected to neighboring satellite cells
subtypes.primary_function: Homeostatic support of ganglion neurons (analogous to astrocytes for DRG neuron soma)

functions: K+ buffering via Kir4.1 (analogous to astrocyte spatial buffering)
Metabolic and nutritional support to ganglion neuron soma
pH regulation
Bidirectional communication with encapsulated neuron via gap junctions, purinergic receptors (P2X, P2Y), and cytokine signaling
Chronic pain sensitization: peripheral injury -> Cx43 gap junction upregulation -> intercellular communication amplifies nociceptive signaling (spread of sensitization)

molecular_markers.name: S100B
molecular_markers.notes: Shared with astrocytes; primary satellite cell marker

molecular_markers.name: GS (Glutamine Synthetase)
molecular_markers.notes: Glutamate-glutamine cycle (analogous to astrocyte function)

molecular_markers.name: GFAP
molecular_markers.notes: Variable expression; upregulated in reactive states

molecular_markers.name: Kir4.1
molecular_markers.notes: K+ buffering (shared mechanism with astrocytes)

molecular_markers.name: Connexin 43
molecular_markers.notes: Gap junctions; upregulated after peripheral injury -> amplifies nociceptive signaling

molecular_markers.name: P2X7
molecular_markers.notes: Purinergic receptor; bidirectional communication with encapsulated neuron

molecular_markers.name: P2Y
molecular_markers.notes: Purinergic receptors; neuron-satellite cell signaling

pain_sensitization.mechanism: After peripheral injury, satellite cells upregulate Cx43 gap junctions, enabling intercellular communication that amplifies nociceptive signaling and spreads sensitization across DRG neurons
pain_sensitization.clinical_relevance: Contributes to chronic pain states; target for DRG stimulator parameter calibration

glial_types.security_implications: DRG stimulators (Abbott Proclaim DRG, Boston Scientific Accuracy) interact with satellite glial cells. Reactive gliosis-like responses and gap junction upregulation occur post-implant, altering stimulation thresholds over time. Understanding satellite cell response is necessary for calibrating DRG stimulator parameters and predicting long-term device efficacy.
